title: Conversation Context Management for GitHub Copilot Chat
body:
<!-- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

**Category:** Chat UX / Developer Productivity  
**Audience:** GitHub Copilot team, VS Code Copilot extension team  

---

## TL;DR

AI coding sessions are session-based by nature, but Copilot Chat treats the context window as a passive scroll buffer with two nuclear options: compact or clear. This proposal introduces **context management** ŌĆö a first-class system for pruning, pinning, snapshotting, and persisting conversation context as structured work product. The core primitive is the **Session Frame**: a bounded, named, exportable unit of context that can be versioned in git, distilled into memory graphs, handed off between agents, and measured with analytics. Tier 1 (selective pruning + pinned turns) can ship independently. The full system closes the loop that currently leaks knowledge at every session boundary.

---

## The Problem

Every AI coding session degrades over time ŌĆö not because the model gets worse, but because the **context window fills with noise it can't ignore**.

Three failure modes, same root cause, different scales:

### 1. The Nuclear Reset (catastrophic)
Context fills mid-task. The only options are "Compact" (lossy summarization you can't control) or "Clear" (wipe everything). You lose the live reasoning ŌĆö the exact variable names, the half-formed plan, the last error message that was about to be relevant. The mental model you've been building across 40 turns vanishes. You start over.

### 2. The Noise Problem (chronic)
Old, irrelevant turns stay in context indefinitely. An early brainstorm about a feature you pivoted away from. A debugging detour that went nowhere. A UI decision that was reversed two turns later. The model can't distinguish "this was overruled" from "this is still true." It confidently reasons from stale context you've mentally discarded.

### 3. The Dead-End Problem (silent)
You explore a direction. It doesn't work. You backtrack ŌĆö but the failed attempt is now permanently woven into your context. There's no way to say "ignore everything from turn 22 onward and try a different approach." You either live with the contamination or reset everything.

### 4. The Lookup Waste Problem (invisible)
An agent runs `semantic_search` across your entire codebase, or calls an MCP tool that fetches 200 records from a database. That result is injected into the context window once ŌĆö and then it's gone. Next session, next task, same topic: the agent runs it again. And again. And again. There is no way to say "you already did this lookup; reuse the result." Every session starts from zero, burning tokens and time on work that was already done.

**These aren't four bugs. They're one missing primitive: context management.**

---

## Proposed Solution

Treat the conversation context window as a **first-class, user-manageable object** ŌĆö not a passive scroll buffer that you periodically nuke.

Borrow the mental model that already works for managing evolving state under complexity: **version control**.

But go further. Developers don't just manage code state ŌĆö they manage **session state**. Every non-trivial coding session has a natural shape: a start, a focus area, a set of discoveries, and an end. That shape is already captured implicitly in the chat transcript. The proposal is to make it explicit ŌĆö to give that shape a name, an interface, and a persistence layer.

---

## The Session Frame Model

Developers naturally code in sessions. There's a "start session" moment and an "end session" moment, and everything in between forms a **frame** ŌĆö a bounded unit of context with a purpose, a topic, and a set of outcomes.

Frames are already how developers communicate. Handoff documents are manually written frames. Session logs are informal frames. Sprint retrospectives are curated frames. The cognitive overhead of maintaining all of this by hand is enormous ŌĆö and entirely avoidable if the tooling understood that sessions have structure.

### What is a Frame?

A frame is a named, bounded segment of a conversation with:
- A **start boundary** (explicit or inferred from topic shift)
- An **end boundary** (session end, checkpoint, or user-defined)
- A **topic label** (`worklog-search`, `auth-bug`, `db-schema-refactor`)
- A **status** (`active`, `closed`, `archived`, `exported`)
- A **set of outcomes** ŌĆö key decisions, code changes, file paths touched, tool results produced

The session currently produces all of this data implicitly. The feature request is to surface it explicitly.

### Frame Operations

| Operation | What it does |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Export** | Serialize the frame to a portable format (JSON, Markdown, structured YAML). Send it to another agent, another tool, or a file. |
| **Compact** | User-directed summarization of a frame: keep the outcomes, discard the reasoning trail. Compaction is always scoped and always previewed before applying. |
| **Prune** | Remove specific turns from a frame without compacting. Surgical, non-destructive. |
| **Archive** | Close a frame without deleting it. It leaves the active context window but remains searchable and reloadable. |
| **Import** | Inject a previously exported frame (or part of one) into a new session's context. |
| **Merge** | Combine the outcomes of two frames into a single context object ŌĆö e.g., merge "auth-redesign" frame findings into the "dashboard-rebuild" frame. |
| **Extract to Memory** | Distill a frame's key entities, decisions, and file paths into a persistent memory graph that survives session reset. |

### Frame-Based Handoffs

Today, AI-assisted developers manually write handoff documents at the end of sessions to preserve context for the next one. These documents are essentially manually authored frames.

With first-class frame support, the handoff becomes **automatic**:
- Session ends ŌåÆ frame is closed and serialized
- Frame includes: topic, decisions made, files touched, open questions, tool results cached
- Next session starts ŌåÆ agent loads the frame as its opening context, not a blank slate
- Frame can be passed to a **different agent entirely** ŌĆö allowing clean task handoffs between specialized agents without context loss

This isn't just a convenience. For teams using multiple AI agents (one for backend, one for frontend, one for review), frames are the **native inter-agent communication protocol**.

---

## Feature Breakdown

### 1. Checkpoints (Snapshots)
> *"Commit" the current state of the conversation.*

- User can mark any point in the conversation as a named checkpoint: `checkpoint: "before refactor"`, `checkpoint: "working login flow"`.
- Checkpoints are visible in a side panel or inline in the chat as labeled markers.
- Restoring a checkpoint restores the model's effective context to that moment ŌĆö subsequent turns are not deleted, just excluded from the context window.
- This is non-destructive. History is preserved; what's *fed to the model* is scoped.

### 2. Selective Turn Pruning
> *"Stage" specific turns for removal from the context window.*

- Right-click any turn ŌåÆ "Remove from context" ŌĆö the message stays in the visual history (grayed out) but is excluded from future requests.
- Multi-select: prune a range of turns at once (e.g., a debugging detour that's now resolved).
- "Prune resolved" shortcut: automatically gray out any turns where the user followed up with "ok, ignore that" or "never mind."
- Pruned turns are visually distinct but never permanently deleted ŌĆö they can be re-added to context at any time.

### 3. Pinned Turns
> *"This turn always stays in context, no matter what."*

- Pin any turn ŌĆö a key architectural decision, a confirmed constraint, a snippet that must be referenced.
- Pinned turns are immune to compaction, pruning, and scroll-off.
- Shown in a dedicated "Active Context" panel so you can see exactly what the model always knows.
- Maximum pin count enforced by token budget (shown as a live indicator).

### 4. Branches
> *"Explore a dead-end without polluting the main thread."*

- "Branch from here" ŌĆö creates a parallel conversation that shares all turns up to that point but diverges from that moment forward.
- Branch is labeled and switchable from a tab or dropdown.
- Branches can be abandoned (archived) or **merged back** ŌĆö cherry-pick specific turns from the branch into the main thread.
- Enables: "let me try approach B without losing approach A."

### 5. Turn Annotations
> *"Teach the model what this turn means."*

- Annotate any turn with a status tag: `Ō£ģ Resolved`, `ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Overruled`, `­¤ōī Key constraint`, `­¤Ü¦ In progress`, `ŌØī Dead end`.
- Annotations are optionally injected as a preamble to every request: *"Note: the following earlier decisions have been overruled: [turns 12, 18, 31]."*
- This is lightweight semantic context ŌĆö no new tokens consumed by restating the content, only by naming the status.

### 6. Tool Result Caching (Topic-Scoped Lookups)
> *"You already did this lookup. Reuse it."*

This is one of the most overlooked sources of token waste in agentic workflows.

When an agent runs an expensive tool ŌĆö a full codebase semantic search, an MCP database query, a web fetch, a dependency graph traversal ŌĆö that result is injected into context once, used, and then lost. The next session that touches the same topic re-runs the tool from scratch.

**Proposed behavior:**
- Any tool call result can be marked as **"cached for topic"** ŌĆö e.g., `topic: auth-module`.
- When a new task arrives that matches the topic, the agent is offered the cached result before re-running the tool.
- Cache entries have a TTL (configurable: by session, by commit SHA, by date, or manual invalidation).
- Cached results are shown in the Active Context panel with a label: `­¤ō” Cached: codebase-scan (auth) ŌĆö 2026-06-01`.
- The agent can reason over cached lookups without re-executing the underlying tool, cutting both latency and token cost.

**Example:**
An agent ran `codegraph_context` across the worklog module and produced a 3,000-token result. That result is now cached under topic `worklog`. Every subsequent worklog task this session ŌĆö or across sessions, if the commit SHA hasn't changed ŌĆö can reference the cached result instead of re-running the graph traversal.

This is **topic-scoped ambient context**: the agent knows the territory without having to rediscover it.

---

## UX Sketch

```

```

---

## Git as the Persistence Layer

Frames are data. Data belongs in version control.

### Frames as Git Artifacts

- When a session frame is closed and exported, it is committed to the repository as a structured artifact ŌĆö e.g., `.copilot/sessions/2026-06-02_auth-redesign.frame.json`.
- The frame file contains: topic, status, timestamps, the full turn log (or a compacted summary), tool results cached, file paths touched, decisions recorded, open questions.
- The frame is committed at the same time as the code changes it produced ŌĆö creating an **unbreakable link between the conversation and the commit**.
- `git log` shows both the code diff and the reasoning that produced it. `git blame` gains a new dimension: not just who changed a line, but **what conversation led to that change**.

### Frame ŌåÆ Commit Linkage

```
git log --frames  (hypothetical)

  2c4f2ba  feat(auth): implement OAuth flow
           Frame: .copilot/sessions/2026-06-02_auth.frame.json
           Topic: auth-redesign | Turns: 38 | Tool calls: 4 cached
           Key decisions: [JWT over session cookies, RS256 signing]
           Open at close: [refresh token rotation ŌĆö deferred]

  1dae6b6  refactor(db): normalize user schema
           Frame: .copilot/sessions/2026-05-31_db-refactor.frame.json
           Topic: db-schema | Turns: 22 | Tool calls: 6
```

This isn't just provenance. It's **institutional memory** ŌĆö the reasoning behind every decision, automatically preserved alongside the code.

### Session Resume from Git

- Checking out an older branch? The frame files from that branch's sessions are available.
- An agent can be instructed: "load the frame from when we last worked on this branch" and resume with the same context that produced the original code ŌĆö without reading the entire codebase from scratch.
- This is the difference between "here's the code" and "here's the code and here's why it works this way."

### Memory Graph Extraction

Frames are the raw material for persistent memory graphs.

- On session close (or on demand), a frame can be **distilled into a memory graph** ŌĆö extracting domain entities, key decisions, file paths, and architectural observations.
- These memory nodes persist across sessions, across branch switches, across team members.
- The memory graph is the compressed, structured, always-available form of every frame ever closed.
- When a new session starts, the agent doesn't load raw frames ŌĆö it loads the memory graph for the relevant topic, which is small, dense, and token-efficient.

```
Frame (raw, 8,000 tokens)
  ŌåÆ Distill
    ŌåÆ Memory graph nodes (300 tokens):
       - "auth module uses RS256 JWT, refresh rotation deferred"
       - "UserSchema normalized 2026-05-31, migration in 20260531_normalize_users.sql"
       - "OAuth callback at /api/auth/callback, handler in src/app/api/auth/route.ts"
```

This is the **closed loop**: sessions produce frames, frames produce memory, memory seeds the next session's context ŌĆö without any manual handoff writing.

---

## Automated Context Pipelines

Once frames are first-class objects with a defined schema, developers can build **automated systems** around them ŌĆö pipelines that treat context as a data product.

### Automated Pruning

- A post-session pipeline reads the closed frame and applies pruning rules: remove turns tagged `ŌØī Dead end`, remove tool call responses beyond a configurable token threshold, remove duplicate reasoning chains.
- Result: a lean, pruned frame that preserves outcomes without preserving the path ŌĆö ready for handoff or memory extraction.
- Rules are configurable per-project (`.copilot/pruning-rules.json`) and can be tuned by the user or auto-learned from their manual prune history.

### Automated Extrapolation for Reusability

- A pipeline scans closed frames for **reusable patterns** ŌĆö repeated tool calls, frequently referenced file paths, recurring architectural questions.
- These patterns are promoted to a "project knowledge base" that's injected at session start for relevant topics, without the user doing anything.
- Example: every session that touches the `auth` module automatically opens with the last 3 key decisions from the `auth` memory graph ŌĆö zero token overhead, maximum relevance.

### Inter-Agent Routing

- A frame can be exported and **handed off to a specialized agent** ŌĆö e.g., the backend agent closes its frame, exports it, and the frontend agent imports just the API contract section as its opening context.
- This makes multi-agent workflows composable: each agent works in its own context frame, and frames are the handoff protocol between them.
- No agent needs the full conversation history of another. It gets the **extracted interface** ŌĆö the decisions and outputs that are relevant to its domain.

### Example Pipeline (developer-defined)

```yaml
# .copilot/pipelines/on-session-close.yaml
on: session.close
steps:
  - prune:
      remove_tagged: [dead-end, overruled]
      max_tool_result_tokens: 1000
  - extract_memory:
      topics: auto-detect
      destination: .copilot/memory/
  - commit_frame:
      path: .copilot/sessions/
      message: "chore(context): close session ŌĆö {topic}"
  - notify:
      if: open_questions > 0
      message: "Session closed with {open_questions} unresolved items"
```

---

## Context Analytics

Once frames are serialized and stored, they become a **data source** ŌĆö and context becomes measurable.

### Metrics That Become Possible

| Metric | What it tells you |
|--------|------------------|
| **Tokens per session by topic** | Which areas of the codebase are most expensive to work in |
| **Tool call repetition rate** | How often the same lookup is run across sessions (measures caching opportunity) |
| **Prune rate by turn type** | What kinds of turns are most often removed ŌĆö signals noisy prompt patterns |
| **Dead-end ratio** | What fraction of explored paths are abandoned ŌĆö proxy for task clarity |
| **Frame-to-commit density** | How many sessions it takes to produce a commit ŌĆö proxy for task complexity |
| **Memory hit rate** | How often an opened session finds relevant memory nodes vs. starts cold |
| **Context freshness** | How old (by commit SHA or date) the cached tool results being used are |
| **Handoff gap** | Time between session close and the next session that opens the same frame ŌĆö measures context decay risk |

### Why This Data Matters

For individual developers: these metrics surface **their own inefficiency patterns**. If your dead-end ratio spikes every time you work on a particular module, that module has a clarity problem ŌĆö not a code problem.

For teams: aggregate context analytics reveal **organizational knowledge debt** ŌĆö the modules where every developer starts from scratch every session, burning tokens on lookups that should be in shared memory.

For tooling builders: context metrics enable **adaptive context management** ŌĆö the system learns what to pin, what to prune, and what to cache based on observed patterns across sessions, not just per-request rules.

### Privacy and Data Ownership

This data is sensitive. It contains reasoning trails, failed approaches, and decision context that developers may not want shared broadly.

- **Local by default**: frame files and analytics live in the repository (or a local `.copilot/` folder), not in a cloud service unless explicitly opted in.
- **User-controlled export**: no frame data leaves the machine without an explicit export action.
- **Opt-in aggregation**: team-level analytics require explicit opt-in at the repository level.
- **Redaction support**: before committing a frame to git, a user can redact specific turns or fields (API keys accidentally pasted, personal notes, etc.).

---

## Why This Matters Beyond One User

This isn't a power-user feature. It's the **missing layer** between "AI pair programmer" and "AI collaborator."

Today, every non-trivial AI-assisted work session requires the user to manually maintain their own external context ŌĆö handoff documents, session notes, re-pasting key snippets at the start of new conversations. This is cognitive overhead that defeats the purpose of the tool. Developers have built entire custom systems to compensate: memory MCP servers, handoff markdown conventions, `.instructions.md` files, prompt templates that reconstruct context at session start. **The tooling has forced users to become their own context managers.**

Long sessions are where the highest-value work happens: architectural decisions, complex debugging, iterative design. These are also the sessions most likely to exhaust the context window and produce the most valuable frames. The users who need Copilot most ŌĆö the ones building complex things over multiple sessions ŌĆö are penalized most by the lack of context management.

Session-based development is how software is actually built. Every sprint is a session. Every feature branch is a frame. Every PR description is a manually written frame summary. The gap is that the tooling treats all of this as informal ŌĆö text written by the developer, disconnected from the conversation that produced it. Making it formal closes that gap entirely.

**The primitive already exists in every other tool that manages evolving state: version control, undo history, layers in design tools, breakpoints in debuggers.** Conversations are state. State deserves management.

---

## Implementation Tiers

Proposing in ascending complexity so the team can ship incrementally:

| Tier | Features | Complexity |
|------|----------|------------|
| **Tier 1** ŌĆö Surgical Control | Selective turn pruning + pinned turns | Low ŌĆö UI-side context filtering before request |
| **Tier 2** ŌĆö Snapshots | Named checkpoints + restore | Medium ŌĆö serialize context state, store per-session |
| **Tier 3** ŌĆö Session Frames | Bounded named frames + export/import/compact | Medium-High ŌĆö frame schema, frame operations, frame panel UI |
| **Tier 4** ŌĆö Tool Result Caching | Topic-scoped lookup caching + TTL management | Medium ŌĆö instrument tool call results, cache with invalidation |
| **Tier 5** ŌĆö Git Integration | Frame ŌåÆ commit linkage, frame artifacts in repo | High ŌĆö git hook integration, `.copilot/sessions/` schema |
| **Tier 6** ŌĆö Memory Extraction | Frame ŌåÆ memory graph distillation | High ŌĆö extraction pipeline, memory graph API |
| **Tier 7** ŌĆö Automated Pipelines | Developer-defined post-session YAML pipelines | High ŌĆö pipeline runner, configurable rules, inter-agent routing |
| **Tier 8** ŌĆö Context Analytics | Metrics dashboard, adaptive context management | Very High ŌĆö data pipeline, aggregation, privacy controls |
| **Tier 9** ŌĆö Branching | Branch + annotate + cherry-pick merge | Very High ŌĆö conversation graph, not just a linear array |

Tier 1ŌĆō2 alone eliminates the Nuclear Reset and Noise problems. Tier 3ŌĆō4 eliminate the Lookup Waste problem. Tier 5ŌĆō6 close the handoff loop. Tier 7ŌĆō9 are the platform.

---

## Prior Art / Analogs

- **Git** ŌĆö the foundational mental model for managing evolving state with branching, commits, and annotation. Frames are to conversations what commits are to code.
- **Jupyter Notebooks** ŌĆö cells as named, reorderable, re-runnable units of reasoning. Frames extend this to conversational turns.
- **Photoshop History panel** ŌĆö non-destructive editing with named snapshots; prune without losing the original.
- **Debugger breakpoints** ŌĆö pin specific execution points for repeated inspection; cache the state at that point.
- **Obsidian Canvas** ŌĆö spatial arrangement of ideas that were previously linear; topic-scoped ambient context.
- **Redis / Memcached** ŌĆö TTL-based caching of expensive computation results. Topic-scoped tool result caching applies the same principle to agent lookups.
- **GitHub Actions** ŌĆö developer-defined automation pipelines triggered by lifecycle events. The post-session pipeline is the same primitive applied to the conversation lifecycle.
- **Linear / Notion** ŌĆö structured work items with provenance and history. Frames formalize the same structure for AI conversations.

None of these solve the AI chat context problem directly ŌĆö but the user intuition for "checkpoint, branch, prune, pin, cache, pipeline" is already widely established across tools developers use every day. This proposal extends familiar patterns into a new medium.

---

## Closing

The context window is the most constrained resource in an AI-assisted workflow, and today it's managed entirely by the model ŌĆö not the user. Giving users first-class control over what the model knows, when it knows it, and what it should forget is not a quality-of-life improvement.

But this proposal is larger than context window management. It's about what happens when you treat a conversation not as a chat log, but as **structured work product** ŌĆö something that can be versioned, persisted, searched, handed off, measured, and automated around.

Developers have been session-based workers since the first terminal. AI coding tools should be session-aware. Frames are the unit. Git is the persistence layer. Memory is the compressed intelligence. Pipelines are the automation surface. Analytics are the feedback loop.

The full system closes a loop that currently leaks knowledge at every session boundary: from conversation ŌåÆ frame ŌåÆ memory ŌåÆ next session's context. No manual handoffs. No token waste on repeated lookups. No dead reasoning contaminating live context. No institutional knowledge is trapped in chat logs that no one can search.

When that loop closes, it's the difference between a tool you use and a system that learns ŌĆö not by training, but by remembering.

---

*Draft by Sory Kaba ŌĆö 2026-06-02*  
*Related: VS Code Copilot Chat, GitHub Copilot, AI pair programming, context window management, agentic workflows, session-based development, memory graphs, automated context pipelines*

title: VS Code 1.122.1: 1M context window option missing for all Copilot Chat models on Copilot Pro+

I checked the model picker and Manage Language Models, and none of the available models show 1M context or any related option. Is this still rollout-gated, plan-gated, or missing for some accounts despite Copilot Pro+?

title: GitHub Copilot Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toString') when resetting.
body:
Many times when I try to reset in the chat history, or merely edit a past prompt and resubmit it, this is what happens:

1. GitHub Copilot asks if I want to remove all subsequent edits. I answer "Yes".
2. GitHub Copilot reverts the files.
3. Then GitHub Copilot displays an error in a toast in the bottom, right-hand corner:
    > Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toString')
4. If I restart VS Code **the files have been reset but not the history**!
5. If I then try to resubmit the previous prompt in the history, the same thing happens:
    > Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toString')

**Analysis:** There is some error occurring, likely because of a non-existent method being called on the wrong object, that is fatal and aborts the history restore point process, resetting (i.e. discarding) edits but leaving conversation history in place.

**Workaround:** When this happens, the following is the only way to get around the problem:

1. Restart VS Code.
2. Click "Restore Checkpoint" at the place before the prompt to resubmit.
3. Restart VS Code again.
4. Resubmit the prompt.

title: environment variables ( in linux ) not fetching from all sources in extension development
body: Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes - VS Code Version: 1.122.1 - OS Version: Linux Mint 21.3 Steps to Reproduce: 1. set some 'environment variables' for testing starting with ENVTEST_ in each of the different sources where linux loads environment variables from, I used the following for demo - in ~/.bashrc ```export ENVTEST_USER_DOTBASHRC="from_user_.bashrc"``` in ~/.profile ```export ENVTEST_USER_PROFILE="from_user_profile"``` in /etc/bash.bashrc ( it is /etc/bashrc In some linux ) ```export ENVTEST_ETC_BASHDOTBBASHRC="from_etc_bash.bashrc"``` in /etc/profile ```export ENVTEST_ETC_PROFILE="from_etc_profile"``` in /etc/environment ```export ENVTEST_ETC_ENVIRN="from_etc_environment"``` 2. create a new extension and inside the activate function ``` export function activate(context: vscode.ExtensionContext) { const envTestVars = Object.keys(process.env).filter(eachEnvVar => eachEnvVar.startsWith("ENVTEST")) console.log(envTestVars) const disposable = vscode.commands.registerCommand('sample-tryout.testExtn', () => { // according change command id in package.json vscode.window.showInformationMessage('extension textExt command just ran - check host editors Debug console'); }); context.subscriptions.push(disposable); } ``` **Issue** - env variable from ~/.bashrc doesn't get loaded in the demo ( as per screenshot ) Some days before, some other file ~/.profile or /etc/environment was not loaded and others worked. Though recently ~/.bashrc seems not to load ------ Note - all the 5 sources get loaded - when running a console ts script and executed via ts-note ( screenshot below ) - or when fetching env variables in the terminal ``` $ env | grep ENVTEST ENVTEST_ETC_BASHDOTBBASHRC=from_etc_bash.bashrc ENVTEST_USER_PROFILE=from_user_profile ENVTEST_ETC_ENVIRN=from_etc_environment ENVTEST_USER_DOTBASHRC=from_user_.bashrc ENVTEST_ETC_PROFILE=from_etc_profile ```

title: Test: Multi-chat support for local sessions in the Agents window
body:
Refs: #319602

- [ ] anyOS
- [ ] anyOS

Complexity: 3

Author: @sandy081

---

## Context

This TPI covers multi-chat support for local sessions in the Agents window. Local sessions can now host multiple chats within the same session, matching the Copilot provider's existing multi-chat capability.

PR: #319315

## Setup

1. Latest VS Code Insiders.
2. Open the Agents Window and start a local chat session.

## What to test

### 1. Creating sub chats

1. Start a local chat session and send a message.
2. Create a new chat within the session (click the "new chat" action on the session).
3. Verify the sub chat is created and linked to the parent session.
4. Send a message in the sub chat and verify it persists.
5. Create multiple sub chats and verify they all appear grouped under the same session.

### 2. Switching between chats

1. With multiple chats in a session, switch between them.
2. Verify each chat retains its own conversation history.
3. Verify the session's status, timestamps, and read state are aggregated across all chats.

### 3. Deleting a sub chat

1. Delete an individual sub chat ŌĆö verify a confirmation dialog appears.
2. Confirm deletion and verify the sub chat is removed but the session and other chats remain.
3. Delete the primary (or last remaining) chat ŌĆö verify the entire session is torn down.

title: Multi-chat support for local sessions in the Agents window
body:
Local sessions (`local-chat`) in the Agents window can now host multiple chats. Users can start follow-up conversations within the same session context.

- **Sub chats:** Create a new chat within an existing local session ŌĆö the child is linked to the primary session and grouped together in the sessions list.
- **Aggregated state:** Status, timestamps, and read state are aggregated across all chats in the group.
- **Delete sub chat:** Deleting an individual sub chat shows a confirmation dialog. Deleting the primary or last remaining chat tears down the whole session.
- **Always on:** Multi-chat is unconditionally enabled ŌĆö no setting required.

title: Test: Background send for new sessions in the Agents window
body:
Refs: #319598

- [ ] anyOS
- [ ] anyOS

Complexity: 3

Author: @sandy081

---

## Context

This TPI covers the new background send feature in the Agents window. Users can now fire off a new session request with `Alt+Enter` (or `Alt`-click Send) and immediately start composing the next one, without waiting for the first session to load.

PR: #319565

## Setup

1. Latest VS Code Insiders.
2. Open the Agents Window.

## What to test

### 1. Background send (Alt+Enter)

1. In the new session view, type a message and press `Alt+Enter`.
2. Verify the new session view resets **immediately** ŌĆö you should not navigate into the started session.
3. Verify the new session view retains its state (selected model, context, etc.) except the query text, which is cleared.
4. Verify the started session appears in the sessions list once it commits.
5. Repeat: send 2ŌĆō3 requests in quick succession with `Alt+Enter` and verify all sessions appear in the list without navigating away from the new session view.

### 2. Background send via Alt-click

1. Type a message in the new session view.
2. Hold `Alt` and click the Send button.
3. Verify the same background send behavior as `Alt+Enter` ŌĆö view resets, state retained (except query text), session appears in the list.

### 3. Foreground send (unchanged behavior)

1. Type a message and press `Enter` (no Alt).
2. Verify it navigates into the started session as before.
3. Click the Send button without holding `Alt` ŌĆö verify same foreground behavior.

title: Background send for new sessions in the Agents window
body:
Add the ability to start a new session in the Agents window **without navigating into it**, so the new session view stays put and the user can immediately compose the next message.

- **Background send (Alt+Enter):** Sends the request and returns to a fresh new session view immediately, without navigating into the started session. The session appears in the sessions list once committed.

title: Test: Sessions grid keyboard navigation and bulk close
body:
Refs: #319596

- [ ] anyOS
- [ ] anyOS

Complexity: 3

Author: @sandy081

---

## Context

This TPI covers new keyboard navigation and bulk-close commands in the Agents sessions grid, bringing editor-like ergonomics to session management.

PR: #319335

## Setup

1. Latest VS Code Insiders.
2. Open the Agents Window and create at least 3ŌĆō4 sessions.

## What to test

### 1. Focus sessions by number (Cmd/Ctrl+1ŌĆō9)

1. With multiple sessions visible in the grid, press `Cmd/Ctrl+1` through `Cmd/Ctrl+9`.
2. Verify each shortcut focuses the corresponding session by position (left-to-right order).
3. Verify the actions are available in the Command Palette as `sessions.focusSessionInGrid1` through `sessions.focusSessionInGrid9`.
4. Verify shortcuts only activate when in the Agents/Sessions window context.

### 2. Close All Sessions (Cmd/Ctrl+K Cmd/Ctrl+W)

1. With multiple sessions open, press `Cmd/Ctrl+K Cmd/Ctrl+W` while a session has focus.
2. Verify all sessions are closed and you return to the new-session view.
3. Verify the command is visible in the Command Palette even when no session has focus.
4. Verify the keybinding only fires when a session has focus.

title: Sessions grid keyboard navigation and bulk close
body:
Add editor-like keyboard ergonomics and bulk-close support to the Agents/sessions grid.

- **Focus sessions by position (Cmd/Ctrl+1ŌĆō9):** Focus the Nth visible session in the grid (left-to-right order)
- **Close All Sessions (Cmd/Ctrl+K Cmd/Ctrl+W):** Remove all visible sessions in a single pass

title: Security: Webviews can trigger arbitrary keyboard shortcuts in the main workbench
body:
The default set of webview message handlers have an event called `did-keydown`:

https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/e4074382086b61bdfb0b6738e0853b2510660d57/src/vs/workbench/contrib/webview/browser/webviewElement.ts#L249-L254

This is used to bring out keydown events from the webview frame so keybindings work when you're focused on the webview:

https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/f9994508a78fc1fcdbc598ea53c9e5a541bfdc7a/src/vs/workbench/contrib/webview/browser/pre/index.html#L904-L905

Unfortunately this can create a bit of a security issue. If there is XSS in a webview or it intentionally allows scripts, it can trigger a series of dangerous keydown events like:

**"Terminal: New Terminal Window"** <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>Shift</kbd>+<kbd>Alt</kbd>+<kbd>`</kbd> followed by changing focus <kbd>F6</kbd> and then **"Terminal: Paste Into Active Terminal"** <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>Shift</kbd>+<kbd>V</kbd>.

I don't know what a good solution is in terms of balancing this issue with usability because it wouldn't be the best user-experience to just have shortcuts not work entirely when you're clicked on a webview.

2. Submit a prompt using `@workspace` or attach a massive amount of context that almost entirely fills the model's maximum allowed context window.

4. Copilot Chat receives `finish_reason: "length"` and immediately crashes, throwing `Error: Response too long.`

title: Show AI credits used when no ULB is set
body:
For users in organizations that don't set a ULB, they currently have no visibility into how many AI credits they consume. This is because we do not have a reliable way to meter their usage against the total pool. However, these users would still like to see their consumption at a high level. This has been a key theme from customers since GitHub migrated to usage based billing on June 1 with both pooled entitlements and ULB. 

We could explore showing these users the aggregate of their AI credit consumption on the same monthly cycle as the other plans. We would want to receive this data from upstream to ensure it is accurate rather than calculating this client side.

title: VS Code agent (chat) window does not have access to Copilot CLI's configured MCP servers
body:
### Problem
MCP servers configured for the **Copilot CLI** (visible via `/mcp`) are not accessible from the **VS Code agent/chat window**. Running `/mcp` in the Copilot CLI shows my servers authenticated (e.g. `linear`, `figma`, plus the built-in `github-mcp-server`), but those same servers/tools are not available to the agent inside VS Code.

### Impact
Without MCP access, the agent window / Copilot CLI integration is effectively unusable for my workflow, since the tools I rely on (Linear, Figma, etc.) can't be called.

### What I'm trying to do
I want the MCP servers configured for Copilot CLI to be available in the VS Code agent window, but I can't find how to set that up.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Configure and authenticate MCP servers in Copilot CLI (`/mcp` shows them as Ō£ō authenticated).
2. Open the agent/chat window in VS Code.
3. MCP tools are not available there.

### Expected
MCP servers configured for Copilot CLI should be usable from the VS Code agent window, or there should be clear documentation on how to configure this.

### Environment
- VS Code Copilot CLI integration
- MCP servers: linear, figma (HTTP, OAuth authenticated), github-mcp-server (built-in)

Expose credit usage for GitHub Copilot Chat sessions in Visual Studio Code
body:
<!-- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->
IŌĆÖd like to request a feature that exposes the number of credits used in GitHub Copilot Chat sessions within Visual Studio Code.

As a GitHub Copilot Enterprise user, I currently donŌĆÖt have a clear way to understand how many credits IŌĆÖve used while working in chat. The only option I currently have is to check the number of credits consumed at the end of a chat. There seems to be no way to access this data programmatically (for example through an API surface). This makes it difficult to track usage, understand consumption patterns, and manage usage expectations over time.

### Requested behavior
Please add a way to access programmatically credit usage in Visual Studio Code for Copilot Chat. For example, APIs could expose:

- Credits used for the current chat session
- Credits used per prompt or response
- A running total for the current day, week, or month.

### Why this would help
For Enterprise users especially, usage transparency is important because:

- we may not have direct visibility into consumption during normal development workflows
- it helps us better understand how our Copilot usage maps to our plan
- it would improve trust and predictability when using chat heavily
- it would help teams and users make more informed decisions about usage

### Suggested outcome
Even a simple usage indicator or link to live credit consumption from within VS Code would be very valuable.

Many thanks in advance.

title: Support Agent window access to WSL workspaces and terminal execution
body:
### Feature request

VS Code's Agent window should be able to operate directly against WSL-based workspaces when the editor is connected through Remote - WSL.

### Why this matters

A large part of my workflow runs inside WSL on Windows. Today, the Agent window can feel limited to the Windows host context, which creates friction when I want it to read files, run commands, or inspect a project that actually lives in WSL.

### Expected behavior

- The Agent window recognizes when the active workspace is in WSL.
- File reads and edits work directly on the WSL filesystem.
- Command execution runs in the WSL shell, not on the Windows host shell.
- Path handling is transparent and consistent for WSL paths.
- The experience feels as seamless as local non-WSL workspaces.

### Example use cases

- Open a Linux project in Remote - WSL and ask the Agent to run tests or start a server.
- Inspect and edit files inside a WSL workspace without manual path translation.
- Use the Agent window without switching back and forth between Windows and WSL terminals.

### Related note

I found a related closed issue about agent plugin hooks failing in WSL due to Windows-style path handling, but this request is broader: I want first-class Agent window support for WSL workspaces and execution, not just a hook-path fix.

title: Agents Removing and reinstalling a marketplace does not update it ŌĆö only manual directory deletion works
body:
<!-- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

When using plugin marketplaces in VS Code, the marketplace catalog is never refreshed automatically. The only reliable way to get an updated catalog is to manually delete the cloned repository from disk, which is completely unintuitive and undiscoverable.

Steps to reproduce

Add a plugin marketplace via settings ("chat.pluginMarketplaces") or via Manage Plugin Marketplaces in the UI
The marketplace repository is cloned locally on first use
The marketplace author publishes a new plugin or updates an existing one
Open the Command Palette ŌåÆ "Manage Plugin Marketplaces"
Select your marketplace ŌåÆ "Remove Marketplace"
Re-add the same marketplace URL
Browse plugins from that marketplace
Expected behavior

Removing and reinstalling a marketplace should fetch the latest catalog, showing any newly added or updated plugins.

Actual behavior

The marketplace catalog is not refreshed. The cloned directory on disk is reused as-is, so the plugin list shown is still the old version. Newly published or updated plugins are not visible.

Why this happens

When a marketplace is re-added, the system checks whether a directory for that repository URL already exists on disk. If it does, it skips cloning entirely and reads the existing (stale) files without performing a git pull. There is no indication in the UI that the catalog may be outdated.

Workaround (unintuitive)

Manually delete the cloned marketplace directory from disk, then remove and re-add the marketplace in the UI. This forces a fresh clone.

The exact directory location depends on your system and VS Code variant, but is typically somewhere under your user data or home folder (e.g. ~/.copilot/ or ~/.vscode/). This workaround is completely undiscoverable for regular users.

Impact

Users have no way to see newly published plugins without manual filesystem intervention
Removing and reinstalling a marketplace ŌĆö the most natural "refresh" action ŌĆö silently does nothing
There is no UI indicator that the cached catalog may be stale
Environment

VS Code (any recent version with plugin marketplace support)
macOS / Linux

title: Prevent Escape key from accidentally closing the askQuestions dialog
body:
<!-- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

### Summary

When the `askQuestions` dialog is focused, pressing `Esc` immediately cancels the
turn. This is easy to trigger accidentally, and doing so loses the in-progress
message and interrupts the conversation. There should be a way to prevent the
`askQuestions` dialog from being cancelled by an accidental `Esc` keypress.

### Current behavior

A single `Esc` keypress while the `askQuestions` dialog is focused cancels the
entire askQuestion dialog (even in multi-question cases), with no confirmation.

### Possible solutions

- A setting to disable `Esc`-to-cancel for the `askQuestions` dialog
- Require a confirmation (or a second `Esc` within a short window) before
  actually cancelling
- Treat the first `Esc` as "defocus the dialog" rather than "cancel the turn",
  so that cancellation requires an explicit action.

title: Windows: PowerShell Store detection throws ENOENT when scanning %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\WindowsApps
body:
<!-- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Do Not Delete This! bug_report_template ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- ­¤Ģ« Read our guide about submitting issues: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Submitting-Bugs-and-Suggestions -->
<!-- ­¤öÄ Search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->
<!-- ­¤¦¬ Test using the latest Insiders build to see if your issue has already been fixed: https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders/ -->
<!-- ­¤ÆĪ Instead of creating your report here, use 'Report Issue' from the 'Help' menu in VS Code to pre-fill useful information. -->
<!-- ­¤ö¦ Launch with `code --disable-extensions` to check. -->
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

<!-- ­¤¬ō If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. -->
<!-- ­¤ōŻ Issues caused by an extension need to be reported directly to the extension publisher. The 'Help > Report Issue' dialog can assist with this. -->
- VS Code Version: 1.122.1
- Commit: 8761a5560cfd65fdd19ce7e2bd18dab5c0a4d84e
- Date: 2026-05-29T09:25:37+02:00
- OS Version: Windows 10 Pro 21H1, build 19043.1052

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Start VS Code on Windows.
2. Open the integrated terminal, or trigger terminal profile detection.
3. Check the VS Code logs.

Actual result:

VS Code logs the following error:

```text
ERR ENOENT: no such file or directory, scandir 'C:\Users\weisen\AppData\Local\Microsoft\WindowsApps': Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, scandir 'C:\Users\weisen\AppData\Local\Microsoft\WindowsApps'
    at async readdir (node:internal/fs/promises:953:18)
    at async P1 (file:///C:/Program%20Files/Microsoft%20VS%20Code/8761a5560c/resources/app/out/main.js:406:97618)
    at async Object.ff (file:///C:/Program%20Files/Microsoft%20VS%20Code/8761a5560c/resources/app/out/main.js:406:97484)
    at async j0 (file:///C:/Program%20Files/Microsoft%20VS%20Code/8761a5560c/resources/app/out/main.js:446:27483)
    at async UL (file:///C:/Program%20Files/Microsoft%20VS%20Code/8761a5560c/resources/app/out/main.js:446:28079)
    at async Rw (file:///C:/Program%20Files/Microsoft%20VS%20Code/8761a5560c/resources/app/out/main.js:446:28352)

Expected result:

If %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\WindowsApps cannot be read during Microsoft Store PowerShell detection, VS Code should ignore that Store PowerShell candidate and continue detecting other available shells, such as Windows PowerShell at:

C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe
Additional notes:

The failing code path appears to be Windows PowerShell profile detection. In the minified main.js, j0() checks:

let r = B(process.env.LOCALAPPDATA, "Microsoft", "WindowsApps");
if (!await nr.existsDirectory(r)) return null;
for (let n of await J.readdir(r)) ...
The existsDirectory check is guarded, but the later J.readdir(r) is not. If readdir throws ENOENT, shell detection aborts before falling back to Windows PowerShell.

A local workaround was to wrap the Store PowerShell readdir call in try/catch and return null on failure, allowing the detector to continue to the normal Windows PowerShell fallback.

title: Agents window: MCP tools are listed/startable but execution fails with "No MCP client found for tool ID"
body:
- Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.50.1
- VS Code Version: 1.122.1
- OS Version: Windows_NT
- Feature: agents window / MCP tools

Steps to reproduce:

1. Open the Copilot agents window.
2. Have MCP servers configured and visible in the agent window customization panel.
3. In that customization panel, open one of the MCP servers and start it.
4. In the agent window, invoke a tool from that MCP server.

Example failing tool calls:
- `ado-on-prem-mcp-wit_get_work_item`
- `molti_agent_mcp-list_available_agent_sessions`

Actual result:
- The MCP servers are visible in the customization panel.
- They can be started from that panel.
- Tool invocation in the agent window still fails immediately with:
  - `MCP server 'ado-on-prem-mcp': Error: No MCP client found for tool ID: ado-on-prem-mcp-wit_get_work_item`
  - `MCP server 'molti_agent_mcp': Error: No MCP client found for tool ID: molti_agent_mcp-list_available_agent_sessions`
- The same MCP tools work in the normal VS Code editor/chat experience.

Expected result:
- If the MCP server is listed in the agent window and can be started there, MCP tool calls from that agent session should execute successfully.
- If starting a server does not attach it to the current agent session, the UI should make that clear and/or require opening a new session.

Notes:
- This does not look like a bad tool argument/configuration issue.
- In the failing session, prompt/skill/agent discovery worked, and normal tool confirmation worked, but MCP execution failed because there was no MCP client bound to the tool call.
- It appears the agent window can see MCP configuration and start the server, but the active agent session may not be getting the MCP client attachment (or not retroactively after startup).
- No sensitive workspace paths or credentials are included here.

title: VS Code 1.122: Chat still requires sign-in when using a configured OpenAI-compatible local network model in VSCode Portable Mode

- VS Code Version: 1.122.1
- OS Version: windows server 2019

## Description

After updating to VS Code 1.122.1, the release notes indicate support for offline usage with local or LAN-hosted models.

I configured a custom OpenAI-compatible model endpoint and the model appears to work correctly. For example, sending a simple prompt such as hello returns a valid response.

However, when I ask the model to perform a Chat task (for example, "write a simple Python script"), VS Code shows a message indicating that I need to sign in before Chat can be used.

## Steps to Reproduce:

1. Install VS Code 1.122.1 (Windows x64 Portable).
2. Enable portable mode by creating a data folder in the VS Code root directory.
3. Configure a custom OpenAI-compatible model endpoint.
4. Verify the model responds successfully to a simple prompt such as hello.
5. Ask the model to generate a simple Python script.

## Expected Behavior

The configured OpenAI-compatible local network model should be used for Chat requests without requiring a Microsoft/GitHub sign-in.

## Actual Behavior

Simple prompts work, but Chat requests trigger a sign-in requirement instead of using the configured model.

## Additional Notes

This is a restricted environment, so I am unable to provide screenshots. The issue is consistently reproducible with the setup described above.

title: Terminal "Rerun Task" button closes the task instead of restarting it
body:
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

Versi├│n: 1.122.1 (user setup)
Confirmar: 8761a5560cfd65fdd19ce7e2bd18dab5c0a4d84e
Fecha: 2026-05-29T09:25:37+02:00
Electron: 39.8.8
ElectronBuildId: 13870025
Chromium: 142.0.7444.265
Node.js: 22.22.1
V8: 14.2.231.22-electron.0
SO: Windows_NT x64 10.0.19045

### Description
The "Rerun Task" button located in the terminal's bottom/status bar (or terminal tab side panel) does not restart the task as expected. Instead, clicking it immediately terminates/closes the active task without triggering a new execution.

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Run any configured task (e.g., a watch task like `npm run dev` or a simple echo task).
2. Look at the terminal panel or the status indicator for that running task.
3. Click on the "Rerun Task" (icon with the circular arrow) button.
4. Notice that the task is immediately killed/closed, but it never starts up again.

**Expected behavior:** The active task should be terminated and immediately restarted in a new execution.

**Actual behavior:** The task is terminated/closed, and the process completely stops without restarting.

title: .mcp.json ignores headers field for HTTP MCP servers
body:
<!-- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

When an HTTP MCP server is configured in a workspace `.mcp.json` file with custom `headers`, the headers are silently discarded. The same configuration works correctly in `.vscode/mcp.json`.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Add an HTTP MCP server with headers to `.mcp.json`:
```json
{
    "mcpServers": {
        "my-server": {
            "url": "https://example.com/mcp",
            "type": "http",
            "headers": {
                "X-Custom-Header": "my-value"
            }
        }
    }
}
```
2. Start the server via MCP: List Servers
3. Observe in trace logs (`Developer: Set Log Level > Trace`) that the custom header is not sent in HTTP requests

The equivalent configuration in `.vscode/mcp.json` works correctly:
```json
{
    "servers": {
        "my-server": {
            "url": "https://example.com/mcp",
            "type": "http",
            "headers": {
                "X-Custom-Header": "my-value"
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## Expected Behavior

Custom headers from `.mcp.json` should be forwarded in HTTP requests, matching the behavior of `.vscode/mcp.json`.

## Related Issues

- #302998
  - This issue reports headers working in user `mcp.json` but not in an extension's `.mcp.json`, and was closed as out-of-scope since `.mcp.json` follows the open-plugins spec which lacks `inputs` support. This captures a different problem: the `.mcp.json` parser discards static `headers` values entirely, even though no `inputs` interpolation is involved. Static headers should work regardless of spec differences

## Environment

- VS Code Version: 1.122.1
- OS: Linux x64

title: Agent host CLI: kill_running_server leaks server node child process after upgrade
body:
## Summary

The CLI agent host supervisor's `kill_running_server` path leaks the underlying server node process when restarting after a `_vscodeUpgrade` RPC. The bash launcher script (`<server>/bin/code-server-<quality>`) is terminated, but the `node ... server-main.js` child it `spawn`'d remains alive as an orphan.

## Repro

Discovered while testing https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/319455 against a remote macOS host using a pinned-old-server CLI:

1. Bring up an agent host supervisor on commit A (e.g. `7090241a056cb49cfe90f58da9a8cc44df9262a9`, protocol 0.2.0).
2. Connect a desktop on commit B (newer protocol 0.3.0). Handshake fails with `UnsupportedProtocolVersion`.
3. Click "Update Server". The supervisor receives the `_vscodeUpgrade` RPC, calls `kill_running_server`, then restarts on commit B.
4. Observe processes on the remote: the bash wrapper for commit A is gone, but its node child (e.g. `~/.<serverDataFolderName>/cli/servers/Insiders-7090241a.../server/node ... server-main.js`) is still alive with `ppid=1`.

In my test run the orphan was pid 230 and survived indefinitely; only the new commit-B server's child was tracked by the supervisor after the restart.

## Suspected cause

`cli/src/tunnels/agent_host.rs::kill_running_server` (or the platform-specific kill helper it delegates to) appears to signal the immediate child only. The server launcher is:

```
<server>/bin/code-server-<quality>      (bash)
  ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ node ... out/vs/server/node/server-main.js   (the server)
```

Bash does not forward `SIGTERM` to its children by default, and we don't wait on it before terminating the new generation, so the node child is reparented to PID 1 and continues running. It does eventually exit when its IPC pipe closes, but in our test it persisted for the duration of the run.

## Suggested fix

A couple of options, in rough order of preference:

1. **Signal the whole process group / job** from the CLI side ŌĆö i.e. set up the server child in its own process group at spawn time (`setsid` / `CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP`) and send `SIGTERM` to the negative pgid on shutdown. Robust regardless of what the launch script does.
2. **Make the launcher forward signals**, e.g. `trap` `SIGTERM` / `SIGINT` in `code-server-<quality>` and re-signal `$!` before `wait`. Cheaper change but doesn't help non-shell launchers.
3. **Replace the bash launcher with a direct exec** so the CLI's child *is* the node server. Simplest of all if there's no other reason to keep the shell shim.

## Scope

- CLI only (`cli/src/tunnels/agent_host.rs` and the server-bin shim at `<server>/bin/code-server-<quality>`).
- Independent of https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/319455; that PR fixes the desktop-side reconnect after upgrade, and is unaffected by whether the old server's node child gets reaped cleanly.
- Probably also fires on any non-upgrade shutdown path that goes through `kill_running_server` (e.g. supervisor exit / quality switch), so the fix is generally useful.

(Written by Copilot)

title: Hide Activity Bar icons in User/Workspace Settings
body:
<!-- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

I use a particular extension that is useful to have in the editor area, but its sidebar panel accessed from its activity bar icon serves no purpose for me. I hide it as soon as I see it, however, I've noticed that I've needed to hide it several times over the course of a few months and years. I switch workspaces often which have their own enabled and disabled extensions, but the activity bar icon's existence isn't consistent with this workspace switching either, it simply seems to re-appear at random points in time.

I would like a setting in VSCode's `settings.json` that allows a user to specify the IDs of activity bar icons in order to hide them permanently. This setting should be automatically updated for the user if they right-click to hide an activity bar icon, unless the setting is in use by the workspace, in which case the workspace setting should be changed instead.

title: Prompt file `model` frontmatter persists model selection after prompt completes
body:
### Problem

When a `.prompt.md` file specifies `model` in its YAML frontmatter (e.g. `model: GPT-5 mini`), the model picker switches to that model for the prompt invocation ŌĆö but it stays on that model after the prompt completes. The user's previously selected model is not restored.

### Expected behavior

The `model` frontmatter should be **session-scoped to that prompt invocation only**. After the prompt finishes, the model picker should revert to whichever model the user had selected before invoking the prompt.

### Why this matters

Teams pin an inexpensive model (like GPT-5 mini) for structured-text prompts such as commit message generation, while using a more capable model for general coding tasks. The current behavior forces users to manually switch back after every prompt invocation, which defeats the purpose of per-prompt model pinning.

### Reproduction

1. Select a model in the chat picker (e.g. Claude Sonnet 4.6).
2. Create `.github/prompts/test.prompt.md`:
   ```yaml
   ---
   model: GPT-5 mini
   ---
   Say hello.
   ```
3. Run `/test` in chat.
4. Observe the model picker now shows `GPT-5 mini` instead of reverting to Claude Sonnet 4.6.

### Proposed solution

Treat the `model` frontmatter as a temporary override: save the current model before the prompt runs and restore it after the prompt response completes.

### VS Code version

Latest stable (June 2026)

title: BYOK Ollama drops reasoning effort metadata returned by /api/show
body:
## Summary

The VS Code Copilot BYOK Ollama provider currently loses reasoning-effort metadata even when the Ollama-compatible endpoint returns it correctly from `/api/show`.

This breaks custom gateways such that already expose:

- `supportedReasoningEfforts`
- `defaultReasoningEffort`
- `supportsReasoningEffort`
- `capabilities.supports.reasoning_effort`

As a result, the model can appear in the picker, but the reasoning selector is not exposed in the UI.

## Affected area

- `extensions/copilot/src/extension/byok/vscode-node/ollamaProvider.ts`

## Current behavior

The Ollama discovery flow calls:

1. `GET /api/version`
2. `GET /api/tags`
3. `POST /api/show`

The endpoint can return reasoning metadata in the `POST /api/show` payload, but the provider does not preserve that metadata consistently when building the BYOK model info.

That means downstream code that already knows how to render the reasoning schema never receives the required values.

## Expected behavior

When `/api/show` returns reasoning metadata, the Ollama BYOK provider should preserve it and pass it through to the BYOK model info so the reasoning-effort selector can be generated normally.

## Reproduction

Use an Ollama-compatible endpoint where `/api/show` returns a payload like:

```json
{
  "supportedReasoningEfforts": ["low", "medium", "high"],
  "defaultReasoningEffort": "medium",
  "capabilities": {
    "supports": {
      "reasoning_effort": ["low", "medium", "high"]
    }
  }
}
```

Then open the BYOK Ollama flow in Copilot.

Observed result:
- the model is listed
- the reasoning selector is missing

Expected result:
- the model is listed
- the reasoning selector is available based on the metadata returned by `/api/show`

## Root cause

The common BYOK model pipeline already supports `supportsReasoningEffort`, and the schema builder already knows how to surface it.

The gap is in the Ollama provider path, which fetches model information from `/api/show` but does not fully preserve the reasoning metadata when storing/building the model info object.

## Proposed fix

In `ollamaProvider.ts`, preserve the reasoning-related fields returned by `/api/show` and forward them into the BYOK model info capabilities.

Concretely, the provider should carry through fields such as:

- `supportsReasoningEffort`
- `supportedReasoningEfforts`
- `defaultReasoningEffort`
- `capabilities.supports.reasoning_effort`

so that `byokModelInfo.ts` can build the configuration schema as intended.

## Validation

A focused regression test can cover this in:

- `extensions/copilot/src/extension/byok/vscode-node/test/ollamaProvider.spec.ts`

with an assertion that reasoning metadata returned by `/api/show` is preserved in the final BYOK model info.

## Context

This was reproduced while integrating an Ollama-compatible BYOK backend. The backend already returns the correct metadata, but the current VS Code Ollama provider drops it before the UI schema layer can use it.

title: Copilot Chat tools picker sometimes switches from global tool selection to session-local minimal tools
body:
Type: Bug

## Summary

In VS Code GitHub Copilot Chat, while using the built-in generic Agent mode without custom agents or prompt files, the tools picker sometimes switches from my global tool selection to a session-local minimal selection.

I normally have around 100 tools enabled globally for the default Agent. The tools picker shows:

> Selected tools are applied globally to all chat sessions using the default agent.

However, after some time, a specific chat session can show only 3 selected tools, with a different message:

> Selected tools were configured only for this chat session.

At that point, built-in tools such as edit / execute / read / search / web, extension tools, and MCP tools are unchecked for that chat session. Manually re-selecting tools fixes the session temporarily, but the issue can reappear.

This is not specific to MCP tools. MCP is just one visible symptom because MCP tools become unavailable along with built-in and extension tools.

## Why this seems different from existing issues

I found related issues, but they do not seem to describe this exact behavior:

- #266058: tool enablement was per-window / not global; fixed by storing global tools selection per profile.
- #269431: MCP/tools were re-selected unexpectedly; closed as duplicate of #266058.
- #272320: per-workspace tool configuration / selections reset between sessions; closed as duplicate of #266058.
- #275628: opening the tools list too early during startup can result in every tool being selected. This is related to tools picker state timing, but the behavior here is the opposite: a specific session is reduced to a minimal tool set.

In my case, the global selection still appears to be intact, but the current chat session has a session-local override with only a few tools enabled.

## Environment

- VS Code: 1.122.1
- VS Code commit: 8761a5560cfd65fdd19ce7e2bd18dab5c0a4d84e
- Architecture: x64
- GitHub Copilot Chat extension: 0.50.1
- OS: Windows 11
- Mode: built-in generic Agent mode
- Not using a custom agent for this chat
- Not invoking a prompt file with a `tools` frontmatter field
- Number of globally selected tools shown in the picker: 106
- Minimal session-local state shown in the picker: 3 selected tools

## Observed UI states

Working/global state:

- Tools picker shows 106 selected tools.
- Message says selected tools are applied globally to all chat sessions using the default agent.
- Built-in groups such as agent, edit, execute, read, search, todo, vscode, web, plus extension/MCP groups, are checked.

Broken/session-local state:

- Tools picker shows 3 selected tools.
- Message says selected tools were configured only for this chat session.
- `agent` remains checked, but edit / execute / read / search / todo / vscode / web / extension/MCP groups are unchecked.
- The agent then cannot use tools that were globally enabled.

## Local state observation

I inspected VS Code global storage and found `chat/selectedTools` in `User/globalStorage/state.vscdb`.

At the time I inspected it, the global state was not minimal:

```text
toolSetEntries total 18 / true 15 / false 3
toolEntries total 142 / true 106 / false 36
```

So the global/default tool selection appears to remain broad. The issue looks like a session-local selected-tools override is being created, restored, or applied unexpectedly for an ordinary Agent chat session.

## Expected behavior

When using the built-in generic Agent mode without custom agents or prompt files, the tools picker should continue to use the global default Agent tool selection unless I explicitly configure tools for the current chat session.

A chat session should not silently switch to a minimal session-local tools selection.

## Actual behavior

A chat session sometimes ends up with a session-local tools selection of only 3 tools. This disables built-in tools, extension tools, and MCP tools for that session, despite the global Agent tool selection being configured with around 100 tools.

## Impact

This breaks Agent workflows unexpectedly. The agent may suddenly be unable to read files, edit files, run commands, search the workspace, use web tools, or call MCP tools. It is also hard to notice until a tool-dependent request fails.

## Workaround

Open the tools picker and manually re-select the desired tools. This works temporarily for the affected session.

## Additional notes

I also have this setting enabled:

```jsonc
"chat.alternativeToolAction.enabled": true
```

I am not sure whether this is related, but the bug appears to involve a mismatch between global tool selection and session-local tool selection.

I can provide screenshots showing the two tools picker states:

1. 106 selected tools with the global-default message.
2. 3 selected tools with the current-chat-session-only message.

title: benchmark: comparar consumo de tokens entre CLI e Code7 (agent host)
body:
## Objetivo

Comparar o custo real de tokens por turno entre o uso direto do `claude` CLI e o uso via Code7 (agent host / claude-agent-sdk), rodando as mesmas prompts nos dois ambientes.

## Motiva├¦├Żo

O Code7 envia um system prompt maior (plugins, skills, agent customizations) que pode encarecer o custo por sess├Żo. Com prompt caching, o impacto se dilui ap├│s o primeiro turno ŌĆö mas precisa ser medido para confirmar se vale mudar o modelo padr├Żo de `claude-opus-4-8` para `claude-sonnet-4-6`.

## Como coletar os dados

### Code7
- Habilitar `chat.agentHost.ipcLoggingEnabled: true`
- Cada turno emite um evento `session/usage` nos arquivos `.jsonl` em `~/.config/VS Code7/logs/*/ahp/`
- Campos: `inputTokens`, `cacheReadTokens`, `outputTokens`, `model`

### CLI
- Rodar prompts via `claude --print "<prompt>"` (modo n├Żo-interativo)
- Capturar uso via `--output-format json` ou via logs do `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR` isolado

## Plano de implementa├¦├Żo sugerido

1. Definir um conjunto fixo de N prompts (ex: 10) de complexidade variada
2. Rodar as N prompts no CLI (`claude --print`) e capturar token usage
3. Rodar as mesmas N prompts no Code7 via IPC e capturar `session/usage`
4. Medir pelo menos 5ŌĆō10 turnos por ambiente para capturar o comportamento p├│s-cache (n├Żo s├│ o cold start)
5. Calcular custo estimado por turno com os pre├¦os atuais de cada modelo
6. Gerar relat├│rio comparativo: CLI vs Code7, cold start vs cache aquecido

## Crit├®rios de sucesso

- [ ] Script automatizado que roda as prompts nos dois ambientes
- [ ] Relat├│rio com custo m├®dio por turno (p├│s-cache) por ambiente
- [ ] Conclus├Żo sobre se vale mudar o modelo padr├Żo para Sonnet 4.6

title: Add fixed OAuth callback port option for MCP servers in Copilot
body:
<!-- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ŌÜĀ’ĖÅŌÜĀ’ĖÅ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

### Feature Request: Add fixed OAuth callback port option for MCP servers in Copilot

#### Problem

When connecting to a remote MCP server that requires OAuth authentication, VS Code Copilot binds to a random ephemeral port for the localhost callback (e.g. `http://localhost:54321/callback`). The MCP spec states that authorization servers *SHOULD* accept any loopback port, but many identity providers require the exact `redirect_uri` (including port) to be pre-registered.

There are a couple reasons this is not always practical:

- Enterprise identity providers (Cognito, Entra ID, Okta in strict mode) enforce exact redirect URI matching as a security policy
- Organizations with compliance requirements may not be able to relax redirect URI validation

#### Proposed Solution

Allow users to specify a fixed callback port in the MCP server's `oauth` configuration:

```jsonc
// .vscode/mcp.json
{
  "servers": {
    "my-server": {
      "type": "http",
      "url": "https://mcp.example.com",
      "oauth": {
        "clientId": "my-client-id",
        "callbackPort": 8080
      }
    }
  }
}
```

When `callbackPort` is set, the OAuth callback listener binds to that specific port instead of an ephemeral one, producing a predictable redirect URI (e.g. `http://localhost:8080/callback`) that can be pre-registered with the identity provider.

#### Other Client Examples

This pattern has been adopted by a handful of clients already:

- **Claude Code** ŌĆö [`--callback-port` flag and `"callbackPort"` in JSON config](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp#use-a-fixed-oauth-callback-port)
- **Kiro CLI** ŌĆö [`"redirectUri"` field in oauth config](https://kiro.dev/docs/cli/mcp/#mcpjson-file)
- **Codex** ŌĆö [`mcp_oauth_callback_port` in config.toml](https://developers.openai.com/codex/mcp#configure-with-configtoml)

title: CoPilot Chat
body:
Type: Performance Issue I had my Copilot credits reset today and I was excited to get back to work. My first task was to fix a regression bug that my Copilot agent introduced. I started a fresh chat (As recommended online). I asked the agent to implement a simple fix. 5 minutes later I had use 25% of my monthly credits. I had selected the so-called "10% cheaper" auto AI agent. After a lonnnnggg profanity laced scream, I kept working and the token usage didn't improve. In less than 1 hour, I used 36% of my monthly AI tokens. A token policy that gave me 40 hours / month of AI agent now gives me <4 hours. This is not an acceptable cost for what is delivered. title: Extension host crash loop on Stable 1.122.1: built-in Copilot Chat 0.50.1 registers undeclared "claude-code" chatParticipant ŌåÆ TypeError: e is not iterable
body:
<!-- Related to #276860, #285730, #286303 ŌĆö all now CLOSED. #276860 (the
canonical) was closed as completed, but it was triaged mainly as a benign
"log message during extension-host init". The report below is the FATAL
variant: an unhandled TypeError that terminates the extension host in a loop
on the latest Stable build, which is a different severity. Recent comments on
#276860 report the same crash is still happening. -->

Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes
<!-- The crash originates from the BUILT-IN "GitHub Copilot Chat" extension, so
disabling third-party extensions / extension bisect does not eliminate it. -->

- VS Code Version: 1.122.1 (commit 8761a5560cfd65fdd19ce7e2bd18dab5c0a4d84e), arm64
- OS Version: macOS 15.7.4 (24G517), Apple Silicon

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Use the latest Stable VS Code 1.122.1 (which bundles built-in `GitHub.copilot-chat` **0.50.1**) on a setup where the Copilot Chat "Claude agent" path is exercised at startup (a `claude-code` chat participant gets registered).
2. Open a window; the extension host activates `GitHub.copilot-chat`.
3. Within seconds the extension host crashes and respawns, looping until VS Code shows: **"Extension host terminated unexpectedly 3 times within the last 5 minutes."**

## What happens

Every extension-host cycle logs two errors, then the host dies with **exit code 5**, respawns, and repeats.

`exthost.log`:

```
[error] Error: chatParticipant must be declared in package.json: claude-code
    at BAt.$registerAgent (vscode-file://vscode-app/Applications/Visual%20Studio%20Code.app/Contents/Resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js:985:71448)

[error] Error: e is not iterable

TypeError: e is not iterable
    at g3e.setItems (/Applications/Visual Studio Code.app/Contents/Resources/app/extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:1167:19963)
    at gp._computeFn (.../extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:1167:19702)
    at gp._recompute (.../extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:1167:11921)
    at gp.get (.../extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:1167:11255)
    at gp.reportChanges (.../extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:1167:497)
    at f3e.endUpdate (.../extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:1167:19461)
    at Xvn (.../extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:1167:19179)
    at gp.recomputeInitiallyAndOnChange (.../extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:1167:1092)
    at kP.init (.../extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:3174:1663)
    at Timeout._onTimeout (.../extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:3:12)
    at listOnTimeout (node:internal/timers:585:17)
    at process.processTimers (node:internal/timers:521:7)
```

`main.log`:

```
[info]  Extension host with pid 69203 exited with code: 5, signal: unknown.
[error] [UtilityProcess id: 1, type: extensionHost, pid: 69203]: crashed with code 5 and reason 'crashed'
```

The `TypeError: e is not iterable` is thrown from inside a `setTimeout` callback (`kP.init` ŌåÆ `setItems`), so it is an **unhandled** exception that terminates the whole extension host. The host respawns, re-runs the same init, and crashes again ŌåÆ 3 crashes in 5 minutes. This is the fatal variant, not just the log line referenced in #276860.

## Root cause (diagnosed)

The built-in `GitHub.copilot-chat` **v0.50.1** is internally inconsistent:

- `extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js` references `claude-code` (6 occurrences, plus a `ClaudeWorkspaceFolderService`) and attempts to register a `claude-code` chat participant.
- `extensions/copilot/package.json` declares only `github.copilot.*` participants (`github.copilot.default`, `github.copilot.editsAgent`, `github.copilot.terminal`, etc.) ŌĆö **`claude-code` is not declared.**

So `$registerAgent` rejects the undeclared `claude-code` participant; the agent/items collection is then left in a state that makes `setItems(e)` receive a non-iterable value, and the unhandled throw kills the extension host.

## Things that do NOT work around it

- `"github.copilot.chat.claudeAgent.enabled": false` ŌĆö no effect (the participant is still registered and the crash still happens).
- `"chat.useAgentSkills": false` ŌĆö no effect.
- Disabling third-party extensions ŌĆö no effect (the crasher is a built-in extension).
- Downgrading the extension is refused: `Extension 'github.copilot-chat' is a built-in extension with version '0.50.1' and cannot be downgraded`.
- 1.122.1 is the latest Stable, so there is no Stable update to move to.

## What fixes it

VS Code **Insiders** bundles `GitHub.copilot-chat` **0.51.2026060101**. With that build the `claude-code` error appears at most once and is **non-fatal** ŌĆö the extension host does not enter the crash loop (0 `crashed with code 5` events across multiple sessions in `main.log`). So the fix appears to already be in the 0.51 line; the problem is that the broken **0.50.1** is welded into the latest **Stable** (1.122.1) as a built-in and cannot be replaced without changing the VS Code build.

## Request

Ship a Stable patch (e.g. 1.122.2) bundling a Copilot Chat build that either declares the `claude-code` chat participant or guards the `setItems` iteration / catches the registration failure, so Stable users on 1.122.1 are not stuck in an extension-host crash loop. Reopening / cross-linking #276860 may also help, since it is closed but still being reported.

title: Model only visible when response completes in auto mode
body: Type: Bug In auto model selection mode, if you hover over the response, it tells you the model that was used. But it only does that after the response fully completes. I'd like to see it while the response is generating (ideally right after the prompt is sent or as soon as the model is known). title: GitHub Copilot sees "compilerError" for diagnostics instead of the "C/C++ IntelliSense" diagnostic source
body: Our C/C++ extension has a bunch of known bugs with invalid IntelliSense errors due a different compiler being used, but Copilot is being temporarily mislead to believe it's a "compilerError", due to this Copilot diagnostic tagging code below and the lack of it being sent the diagnostic "source" (our current "source" is "C/C++" but I attempted to fix it by changing it to "C/C++ IntelliSense, but Copilot doesn't see that still). Copilot may be able to determine the source from the error text itself given knowledge of what errors the compilers produce, but it's theoretically possible for some error text to be identical without the "source" to differentiate and seems like it could potentially waste tokens. https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/c2f5ceaea30590a330319fb3a769fb953948fd30/extensions/copilot/src/extension/prompts/node/inline/diagnosticsContext.tsx#L94 A potential fix might just be to add the vscode.Diagnostic source to the context. ...and maybe the "compilerError" text should also be renamed to something less specific and not potentially incorrect.
